subject: Chemistry
q:
The wave function Ψ of 2 s is given by

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r=r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0
question:
The wave function Ψ of 2 s is given by

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r=r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0
solution:
The wave function (Ψ) of 2s is given by,

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r = r0, radial node is formed.

Ψ2s2 = Probability density of finding an electron

At node, Ψ2s2 = 0 (∵Probability of finding an electron is zero at the node)

Ψ2s2 =122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a02

⇒ 0 =18π1a0 2-r0a02e-2r0/a0

⇒ 2 - r0a0 = 0

⇒ 2 = r0a0

⇒ 2a0 = r0

So, the correct option is D.

q: The de-Broglie's wavelength of an electron in the \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit is \(\qquad\) \(\pi \mathrm{a}_0 \cdot\left(\mathrm{a}_0=\right.\) Bohr's radius \()\)
question: The de-Broglie's wavelength of an electron in the \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit is \(\qquad\) \(\pi \mathrm{a}_0 \cdot\left(\mathrm{a}_0=\right.\) Bohr's radius \()\)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 2 \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{n} \lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & 2 \pi \mathrm{a}_0 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{Z}}=\mathrm{n} \lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & 2 \pi \mathrm{a}_0 \frac{4^2}{1}=4 \lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \\ & \lambda_{\mathrm{d}}=8 \pi \mathrm{a}_0\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The de Broglie wavelength of a car of mass 1000 $\mathrm{kg}$ and velocity $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ is :
question: The de Broglie wavelength of a car of mass 1000 $\mathrm{kg}$ and velocity $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ is :
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { } \lambda=\frac{h}{m v} \\
& h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s} \\
& m=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \\
& v=36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=\frac{36 \times 10^3}{60 \times 60} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec} \\
& \therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{10^3 \times 10}=6.6 \times 10^{-38} \mathrm{~m} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Arrange the following orbitals in decreasing order of energy.

A. n=3, l=0, m=0
B. n=4, l=0, m=0
C. n=3, l=1, m=0
D. n=3, l=2, m=1

The correct option for the order is:
question:
Arrange the following orbitals in decreasing order of energy.

A. n=3, l=0, m=0
B. n=4, l=0, m=0
C. n=3, l=1, m=0
D. n=3, l=2, m=1

The correct option for the order is:
solution:
In multi-electronic species, energy is decided on the basis of (n+l) rule.

3s = 3 + 0 + 0= 3

4s = 4 + 0 +0 = 4

3p = 3 + 1 +0 = 4

3d = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6

For 4s and 3p, an orbital with a higher value of n has higher energy.

So increasing the order of energy is 3d>4s>3p>3s.

subject: Chemistry
q: The energy of first (lowest) Balmer line of H atom is $x \mathrm{~J}$. The energy (in J) of second Balmer line of H atom is :
question: The energy of first (lowest) Balmer line of H atom is $x \mathrm{~J}$. The energy (in J) of second Balmer line of H atom is :
solution:
First Balmer line:
transition n=3 to n=2, energy $E_1 = 13.6(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9}) = 13.6 \times \frac{5}{36} = x$ J.
Second Balmer line:
transition n=4 to n=2, energy $E_2 = 13.6(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{16}) = 13.6 \times \frac{3}{16}$ J.
Ratio: $\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{\frac{3}{16}}{\frac{5}{36}} = \frac{3}{16} \times \frac{36}{5} = \frac{27}{20} = 1.35$.
Thus second Balmer line energy is $1.35x$ J.

question: For emission line of atomic hydrogen from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the plot of wave number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>-</mo></mover></mfenced></math> against <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mfenced></math> will be: (The Rydberg constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msub></math> is in wave number unit)
solution:
According to Rydberg's equation,



1λ=v -=RHZ21n12-1n22

=RH×12(1nf2−182)

v¯=RHnf2−RH64

slope, m=+RH

The curve is linear

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R :

Assertion A: In the photoelectric effect, the electrons are ejected from the metal surface as soon as the beam of light of frequency greater than threshold frequency strikes the surface.

Reason R : When the photon of any energy strikes an electron in the atom, transfer of energy from the photon to the electron takes place.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R :

Assertion A: In the photoelectric effect, the electrons are ejected from the metal surface as soon as the beam of light of frequency greater than threshold frequency strikes the surface.

Reason R : When the photon of any energy strikes an electron in the atom, transfer of energy from the photon to the electron takes place.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is not correct
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is not correct but R is correct
solution: The threshold frequency is the minimum frequency required for emission of electrons from the outermost shell. The light with frequencies lower than threshold would not eject photoelectrons from the shell of an atom. As for the emission kinetic energy of an electron should be greater than or equal to zero. Hence, the photoelectric emission will take place only when the energy of a photon is greater than the energy possessed by the electron. Atoms are ionised resulting in transfer of energy when photons of sufficient energy strike the metal surface.

subject: Chemistry
q: The work functions of two metals $\left(M_{A}\right.$ and $\left.M_{B}\right)$ are in the $1: 2$ ratio. When these metals are exposed to photons of energy 6 eV, the kinetic energy of liberated electrons of $M_{A}: M_{B}$ is in the ratio of $2.642: 1$. The work functions (in eV) of $M_{A}$ and $M_{B}$ are respectively.
question: The work functions of two metals $\left(M_{A}\right.$ and $\left.M_{B}\right)$ are in the $1: 2$ ratio. When these metals are exposed to photons of energy 6 eV, the kinetic energy of liberated electrons of $M_{A}: M_{B}$ is in the ratio of $2.642: 1$. The work functions (in eV) of $M_{A}$ and $M_{B}$ are respectively.
solution:
The work functions satisfy $W_A : W_B = 1:2$.
Let $W_A = W$ and $W_B = 2W$.
Using the photoelectric equation
$KE = h\nu - W$, we have $KE_A = 6 - W$ and $KE_B = 6 - 2W$.
Given the kinetic energy ratio $\frac{KE_A}{KE_B} = 2.642$, we get $\frac{6-W}{6-2W} = 2.642$.
Solving: $6 - W = 2.642(6 - 2W)$ gives $6 - W = 15.852 - 5.284W$
So $4.284W = 9.852$ and $W = 2.3$ eV.
Thus $W_A = 2.3$ eV and $W_B = 4.6$ eV.

solution:
$\bar{v}=\mathrm{RZ}^2\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right)$
$$
=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right)=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}
$$

q:
The electron in the nth  orbit of Li2+  is excited to (n+1) orbit using the radiation of energy 1.47×10-17 J (as shown in the diagram). The value of n is ___________

Given : RH=2.18×10-18 J
question:
The electron in the nth  orbit of Li2+  is excited to (n+1) orbit using the radiation of energy 1.47×10-17 J (as shown in the diagram). The value of n is ___________

Given : RH=2.18×10-18 J
solution:
The difference energy between two energy levels is 

∆E=2.18×10-18 1n12-1n22 J

1.47×10-17=2.18×10-18×(9)1(n)2-1(n+1)2

0.749=1n2-1(n+1)2

n = 1

Hence, the transition is taking place between 1→2, and it comes under Lymann series.

If we take 0.749≃0.75=34

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given,
(A) $\mathrm{n}=5, \mathrm{~m}_{1}=-1$
(B) $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{l}=2, \mathrm{~m}_{1}=-1, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{s}}=+\frac{1}{2}$
The maximum number of electron(s) in an atom that can have the quantum numbers as given in (A) and (B) respectively are :
question:
Given,
(A) $\mathrm{n}=5, \mathrm{~m}_{1}=-1$
(B) $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{l}=2, \mathrm{~m}_{1}=-1, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{s}}=+\frac{1}{2}$
The maximum number of electron(s) in an atom that can have the quantum numbers as given in (A) and (B) respectively are :
solution:
For case (A) with n=5, $m_l=-1$:
The principal quantum number n=5 allows l from 0 to 4. Since $m_l=-1$, we need l≥1. For each l value (1, 2, 3, 4), the orbital $m_l=-1$ exists, giving 4 orbitals. Each orbital holds 2 electrons (spin up and down), so maximum electrons = 8.
For case (B) with n=3, l=2, $m_l=-1$, $m_s=+\frac{1}{2}$:
These quantum numbers completely specify a single electron in a unique orbital with a specific spin, so maximum electrons = 1.
Therefore, the answer is 8 and 1.

Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution: $2 p_{\mathrm{x}}$ and $2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}$ are degenerated orbitals hence having equal energy and therefore no spectral line will be observed for $2 p_x \rightarrow 2 p_y$ transition.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following set of quantum numbers.

  n l m1
A 3 3 -3
B 3 2 -2
C 2 1 +1
D 2 2 +2

The number of correct sets of quantum numbers is
question:
Consider the following set of quantum numbers.

  n l m1
A 3 3 -3
B 3 2 -2
C 2 1 +1
D 2 2 +2

The number of correct sets of quantum numbers is
solution:
For a given 'n' value l will have 0 to n-1 values and for a given 'l' value m will have -m to +m values

For n=3, l=3, is not possible.Similarly for n=2, l=2 is also not possible, as n and l have same values.

So, set A, D are not possible.

If n=3, l can have 0 to 2 values and 'm' can have -2 to +2 values,

So, set B is possible.

If n=2, 'l' can have 0 and 1 values and 'm' can have -1 to +1 values.

So, set C is also possible.

Hence, set B and C are the correct set of quantum nmbers.

q: The wavelength of the radiation emitted, when in hydrogen atom electron falls from infinity to stationary state 1 , would be (Rydberg constant $\left.=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)$
question: The wavelength of the radiation emitted, when in hydrogen atom electron falls from infinity to stationary state 1 , would be (Rydberg constant $\left.=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)$
solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]$
$=1.097 \times 10^7\left(\frac{1}{1}\right)$
$\lambda=\frac{1}{1.097} \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$

q: Wavenumber for a radiation having \(5800 Ã…\) wavelength is \(x \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\). The value of \(x\) is ________ (Integer answer)
question: Wavenumber for a radiation having \(5800 Ã…\) wavelength is \(x \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\). The value of \(x\) is ________ (Integer answer)
solution:
$\bar{v}($ wave no. $)=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{5800 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}}=17241$
OR
$1724 \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=1724$
